title: 17.4 — std::array of class types, and brace elision

content.text: Author’s noteWe’ll use structs to illustrate our points in this lesson. The material applies equally well to classes.
We’ll use structs to illustrate our points in this lesson. The material applies equally well to classes.

content.code:
House number 13 has 7 rooms.
House number 14 has 10 rooms.
House number 15 has 8 rooms.

content.text: Author’s noteWe haven’t covered C-style arrays yet, but for the purposes of this lesson, all you need to know is thatT implementation_defined_name[N];is a fixed-size array of N elements of type T (just likestd::array<T, N> implementation_defined_name;).We cover C-style arrays in upcoming lesson17.7 -- Introduction to C-style arrays.

We haven’t covered C-style arrays yet, but for the purposes of this lesson, all you need to know is thatT implementation_defined_name[N];is a fixed-size array of N elements of type T (just likestd::array<T, N> implementation_defined_name;).We cover C-style arrays in upcoming lesson17.7 -- Introduction to C-style arrays.
We cover C-style arrays in upcoming lesson17.7 -- Introduction to C-style arrays.

Key insightWhen initializing astd::arraywith a struct, class, or array and not providing the element type with each initializer, you’ll need an extra pair of braces so that the compiler will properly interpret what to initialize.This is an artifact of aggregate initialization, and other standard library container types (that use list constructors) do not require the double braces in these cases.

When initializing astd::arraywith a struct, class, or array and not providing the element type with each initializer, you’ll need an extra pair of braces so that the compiler will properly interpret what to initialize.This is an artifact of aggregate initialization, and other standard library container types (that use list constructors) do not require the double braces in these cases.
This is an artifact of aggregate initialization, and other standard library container types (that use list constructors) do not require the double braces in these cases.

content.code:
You found: Joe
You found: nobody

A sword costs 5 gold.
A dagger costs 3 gold.
A club costs 2 gold.
A spear costs 7 gold.

content.text: Because we are explicitly specifying the element type for each initializer, we can use CTAD and single braces (due to brace elision) here.
Because we are not explicitly specifying the element type for each initializer, we can’t use CTAD nor brace elision. That means we must use double-braces.
